title: Expressing the sense that there should be established a "National Garifuna Immigrant Heritage Month" in April to celebrate the great contributions of Americans of Garifuna immigrant heritage in the United States who have enriched the history of the Nation.
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make April a special month to honor Garifuna Americans and their contributions to the United States. The Garifuna people have roots from both West Africa and native Caribbean peoples. In 1797, they were moved from the Caribbean to Central America, and later many came to live in the United States. The bill wants to celebrate how Garifuna Americans have helped make our country's history richer and better.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses that Congress believes there should be a National Garifuna Immigrant Heritage Month in April. This month would celebrate the great contributions that Americans of Garifuna immigrant heritage have made to the United States and how they have enriched our nation's history.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: The history of Americans of Garifuna immigrant heritage is deeply connected to the story of our nation. These Americans come from Central America and share a common heritage and language that crosses national borders. Americans of Garifuna heritage came to the United States by choice. They are different from but have similar origins to most African Americans, as both groups are descendants of mostly West and Central Africans who were brought to North America against their will through the historic Atlantic slave trade. The West African slaves who escaped to an island eventually married with the Caribs and Arawaks Indigenous native people. This created what we now know as the Garifuna people. After losing a battle against the British, the defeated Garifuna leaders were taken to England and the rest of the Garifuna people were moved to the island of Roatan in Honduras. They arrived there on April twelfth seventeen ninety seven, which began their settlement in Central America. This year marks the two hundred twenty eighth anniversary of the Garifuna people's arrival in Central America. A National Garifuna Immigrant Heritage Month would give us a chance to remember and recognize the contributions of the Garifuna people and highlight the history and culture of Central American Black Indigenous immigrants in the United States. The language, dance, and music of the Garifuna people from Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua have been added to a List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. This list was first created in two thousand one by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization. The House of Representatives recognizes that the celebration of Garifuna Heritage Month started in New York City in two thousand nine and in New York State in two thousand ten. This happened thanks to the dedicated efforts of community leaders who worked hard to get this recognition. April became the month to honor and celebrate Garifuna culture, history, and contributions. People should know about the Garifuna people's fight for land rights against tourism companies, developers, and drug traffickers in several regions including Pintadillera, Playa Blanca, Puerto Grande, La Flor, Ciudad Morazan which is also called Choloma, Orquidea which is in Colon, and Prospera which is in Islas de la Bahia. Raising awareness about Garifuna immigrant heritage is important for effectively fighting unfair treatment and injustices against Black and Indigenous populations in the United States, Latin America, and around the world.

sections.title: House of Representatives Decision
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives believes that a National Garifuna Immigrant Heritage Month should be established to celebrate the significant contributions of Americans of recent Garifuna immigrant heritage to the history of the United States. The people of the United States should observe this month with appropriate ceremonies, celebrations, and activities to recognize that Americans of recent Garifuna immigrant heritage make our country more diverse and stronger.
